Maddison GDP Explorer

275 years of global economic history · 199 countries · 1750–2024
An interactive visualization and analytical toolkit built on an extended version of the Maddison Project Database, providing long-run estimates of GDP per capita (1750–2024). Includes derived indicators such as growth rates, world rankings, convergence analysis, and cross-country comparisons. Originally developed by Juan Infante-Amate (University of Granada) for teaching and research purposes.
For more information on data sources, curation methodology, derived calculations, and citations, see About.

GDP per capita (2011 USD PPP) iReal GDP per capita in 2011 international dollars adjusted for purchasing power parity. Source: Maddison Project Database 2023.
World ranking (GDP per capita) iPosition among all countries with available data for each year, ranked by GDP per capita. Lower = richer.
Ratio vs World average iCountry's GDP per capita divided by the world average. Values above 1.0 indicate above-average income; below 1.0 indicate below-average.
Growth rate (CAGR 25y) iCompound Annual Growth Rate over rolling 25-year windows. Shows the average annual growth rate for each period.
IndicatoriGDP per capita in 2011 international dollars (PPP). Ratio modes show each country relative to a benchmark: blue = above, red = below.

About this tool

An interactive visualization and analytical toolkit built on the Maddison Project Database, providing long-run estimates of GDP per capita and total GDP across the world. Tool originally developed by Juan Infante-Amate (University of Granada) for teaching and research purposes.

Beyond the original Maddison data, this tool computes a range of derived indicators: compound annual growth rates (CAGR) over customizable periods, world rankings by GDP per capita and total GDP, ratios against the world average or user-defined benchmarks, and convergence/divergence analysis between countries. These post-calculations are produced by the tool itself and are not part of the original database.

The Maddison Project Database

The Maddison Project Database builds on the pioneering work of the economic historian Angus Maddison, who spent decades compiling estimates of economic performance for countries across the world over the very long run. The database provides comparable GDP per capita estimates expressed in 2011 international dollars (PPP), enabling cross-country and cross-time comparisons of living standards.

The latest release (2023) covers 169 countries with varying temporal coverage — some series begin as early as the year 1 CE, while most countries have data from 1820 onwards. The database is maintained by the Groningen Growth and Development Centre (GGDC) at the University of Groningen.

When using this data, the Maddison Project explicitly requires the following citation:

Required citation

Bolt, Jutta and Jan Luiten van Zanden (2024), “Maddison style estimates of the evolution of the world economy: A new 2023 update.” Journal of Economic Surveys, 1–41. DOI: 10.1111/joes.12618

When using subsets of fewer than 12 countries or displaying data graphically, the original source papers for those estimates should also be cited. Consult the source sheet within the database for details.

Data viewing modes

This dashboard offers two data viewing modes, selectable via the toggle at the bottom of every page:

Full Data (default)
Shows all 199 countries for every year from 1750 to 2024. Includes original Maddison data, World Bank trends, growth extrapolations, and regional average estimates. Ideal for exploring global patterns, visualizing maps, and educational use. Gaps have been filled using the methodology described below.
Maddison/WB Original
Shows only high-reliability data: original Maddison Project estimates and World Bank growth trends (2019–2024). Excludes all gap-filled values (growth extrapolations and regional averages). Recommended for research and rigorous analysis where data provenance matters. Countries and years without original data will appear blank.

The toggle applies globally to all views: globe, map, charts, rankings, tables, and CSV exports. Look for the Full Data | Maddison/WB Original selector in the footer bar.

Data curation for this dashboard

The original Maddison database covers 169 countries but with an unbalanced panel: coverage varies enormously across countries and periods. Many nations have data only from 1950, while others go back centuries. For this dashboard, we extended coverage to all 199 UN-recognized countries and filled temporal gaps to build a complete 1750–2024 panel. This allows map and globe visualizations to display all countries for every year, but it comes at a cost: many observations are estimates rather than direct historical data.

We therefore urge caution when using this dashboard for research purposes. The filled values are approximations intended for educational visualization, not substitutes for primary data. For research, always consult the original Maddison database.

Population data comes from Our World in Data, used to compute GDP totals (GDP per capita × population) and for population-weighted regional aggregates.

Gap-filling methodology

Gaps are filled using a hierarchical approach that prioritizes country-specific data. Where possible, World Bank growth trends are used to extend Maddison series using each country’s own observed trajectory. For remaining gaps, regional growth patterns are applied. Every observation is tagged with a reliability indicator so users always know the quality of what they are seeing:

Original — high reliability
Direct data from the Maddison Project Database, grounded in national accounts, historical records, and expert estimates compiled by economic historians. Example: United Kingdom from 1750, United States from 1800, Japan from 1870.
WB trend — high reliability
Estimates derived from World Bank growth trends (2019–2024) applied to the nearest available Maddison benchmark. Uses each country’s own observed GDP growth trajectory, providing a country-specific (rather than regional) estimate.
Growth extrapolation — medium reliability
Where a country has some Maddison data but not for all years, we extrapolate using population-weighted growth rates from its region (UN sub-region → Maddison region → UN region). The country’s known data points anchor the estimate, and the regional growth trajectory fills the gaps. Example: Colombia has Maddison data from 1820; its 1750–1819 values are estimated using South American growth trends.
Regional average — low reliability
For countries with no Maddison data at all (mostly small nations not in the original 169-country dataset), we assign the population-weighted average of their region for each year. This provides a plausible order-of-magnitude estimate but carries substantial uncertainty. Example: Tuvalu, Palau, and other Pacific island nations not covered by Maddison use the Oceania regional average.

Special treatment for resource-wealth economies: Countries whose 1950 GDP per capita already exceeded the Western European average due to natural resource wealth (primarily Middle Eastern oil producers) receive adjusted pre-resource estimates. Their pre-1950 values are anchored to the global non-Western average in 1850 and filled using neighboring non-resource economies’ growth patterns, preventing the projection of oil-era wealth into pre-industrial periods.

Regional aggregates (Maddison regions, Minerva regions, UN sub-regions, World) are computed as population-weighted averages for per capita values and sums for totals, always from the country-level data.

Explore country data quality

Select a country to view its full GDP per capita series, colored by the reliability of each observation. The bar below shows the proportion of original, extrapolated, and estimated data.

Original WB trend Growth extrap. Regional avg.

Solid line = original Maddison data or WB trend · Dashed line = growth extrapolation · Dotted line = regional average

Credits & how to cite this tool

This interactive dashboard was developed by Juan Infante-Amate, Department of Economic History, University of Granada (Spain). The data curation pipeline, gap-filling methodology, and web visualization were designed for educational use in economic history courses.

Infante-Amate, J. (2025). Maddison GDP Explorer: An interactive visualization of the Maddison Project Database 2023. University of Granada. Available at:

Please always cite both this tool and the original Maddison Project Database (see above) when using or referencing this visualization.
